title: Human Approval Boundary Packet
summary: Notes about when AI-generated operational recommendations can act automatically versus require review.

sources.title: Governance workshop
sources.excerpt: Participants approved automatic drafting, summarization, and artifact linking when outputs are clearly marked as generated. They rejected automatic policy changes, customer commitments, and release blocking without a human approver.

sources.title: Operations incident note
sources.excerpt: A prior automation posted a recommendation as if it were an approved decision, causing two teams to pause rollout work unnecessarily. The incident review asks for explicit status labels: draft, recommendation, approved action, and blocked.

sources.title: Product analytics
sources.excerpt: Users are most likely to accept generated briefings when the interface separates evidence, recommendation, and action controls. Acceptance drops when the recommendation and execution button appear in the same visual cluster.

sources.title: Legal review
sources.excerpt: Any automated message that could be interpreted as a customer commitment needs an accountable human reviewer. Legal approved internal draft summaries as long as they cannot be confused with final commitments.
